headline_summary: Reuters (Apr 8): U.S., Iran (and Israel) announce a two-week ceasefire brokered by Pakistan; talks slated to pursue a longer-term settlement.
Reuters (Apr 8): Iran says Strait of Hormuz transit remains closed without a permit; shipowners seek clarity and major liners stay cautious.
Reuters (Apr 7): U.S.-Israeli war with Iran has damaged 40+ energy assets and disrupted ~20% of global oil/LNG flows via Hormuz; U.S. issues ultimatum to reopen.
Reuters (Apr 7): Iran’s Supreme National Security Council outlines ceasefire-related demands (sanctions lifting, enrichment acceptance, U.S. withdrawal) and signals continued leverage over Hormuz.
Reuters (Apr 6/4): Drone strikes in southern Iraq (Basra/Rumaila) highlight spillover risk to non-Iranian supply and services operations.
Reuters (Apr 5): Kuwait reports severe damage after Iranian drone attack on oil facilities; recovery could take months.
Reuters (Apr 5): OPEC+ agrees a largely ‘on paper’ quota increase for May; additional barrels are irrelevant while Hormuz remains disrupted.
Reuters (Apr 3): Limited neutral/friendly transits (CMA CGM; Japanese LNG carrier; Omani tankers) show selective passage policy rather than full reopening.
Market focus: war-risk insurance, freight, and physical supply timing (6–8+ weeks to normalize) keep oil volatility and risk-off sensitivity elevated.
risk_drivers: Hormuz transit still requires Iranian permits; closure can re-tighten quickly
Ceasefire is time-limited (two weeks) and contingent; negotiations face major gaps (enrichment, missiles, sanctions)
Spillover attacks on Gulf and Iraqi energy infrastructure (drones/missiles) reduce effective supply capacity
LNG disruption (Qatar force majeure) and refinery outages raise global energy price tail-risk
Maritime incident count and threats (target ships without permission) sustain war-risk premia
OPEC+ spare capacity cannot reach market if shipping chokepoints remain impaired

Energy: oil_up_risk: Permit-based Hormuz passage + infrastructure damage keeps upside skew in crude and product cracks.
Freight/insurance: war-risk premia likely persist; normalization could take 6–8+ weeks even if ceasefire holds.
Gas/LNG: Qatar force majeure and facility damage keep LNG tightness and price volatility elevated.
FX: em_fx_pressure: Higher energy import bills and inflation pass-through pressure oil-importer EM FX.
risk_off_flows: Escalation headlines support USD/CHF/JPY; higher vol for high-beta and carry FX.
Rates: rates_credit_spreads: Risk-off and higher energy-driven inflation expectations can widen HY/EM spreads and lift breakevens.
Ceasefire progress could compress near-term risk premium, but term premium remains sensitive to Hormuz access and supply repair timelines.
Equities: risk_off_flows: Fuel- and freight-sensitive sectors (airlines, shipping, consumer cyclicals) face margin risk; energy/defense relatively supported.
GCC/Iraq: Higher oil can support revenues, but security risk and operational disruptions weigh on valuations and capex cadence.

sources.title: What the US, Iran, Israel and Pakistan have said about the ceasefire
Shippers seek clarity on Hormuz passage as Iran issues fresh warnings
How the US-Israeli war with Iran is disrupting oil and gas
OPEC+ agrees to boost oil output when Strait of Hormuz reopens

events.title: U.S., Iran and Israel announce a two-week ceasefire brokered by Pakistan; talks planned to pursue longer-term settlement
events.implication: Reduces immediate escalation risk but remains time-limited and contingent; market risk premia can fade quickly if implementation holds, but tail-risk persists around negotiation gaps.

events.title: Iran warns Strait of Hormuz remains closed to vessels without permits; shippers and major liners remain cautious
events.implication: Permit-based access sustains war-risk insurance and freight premia and keeps physical oil/LNG flows vulnerable to renewed stoppages.

events.title: U.S. strikes military targets at Kharg Island as war-driven energy disruptions mount (IEA cites 40+ damaged assets)
events.implication: Elevates risk of further supply outages and retaliation affecting Gulf energy infrastructure; reinforces oil upside tail-risk and risk-off episodes.

events.title: Drone strike hits BP-operated North Rumaila oilfield; three workers wounded (no immediate operational impact reported)
events.implication: Demonstrates spillover risk to Iraqi supply chain and services; can delay recovery even if Hormuz access improves.

events.title: Kuwait Petroleum Corporation reports severe material damage after Iranian drone attack causes fires at operating units
events.implication: Reduced refining/output capacity tightens regional product balances and increases duration risk for supply normalization.

events.title: Ceasefire positions reveal key negotiating demands: Iran seeks sanctions lifting and recognition of enrichment; U.S. reiterates constraints (HEU removal, halt enrichment, missiles)
events.implication: Signals slow, contentious talks and policy uncertainty around sanctions and enforcement—supporting persistent geopolitical risk premium in energy and EM assets.

events.title: Selective ‘friendly’ transits continue: Japanese LNG carrier, French-owned CMA CGM ship, and Omani-operated tankers cross the Strait of Hormuz
events.implication: Indicates conditional reopening via bilateral coordination rather than restored freedom of navigation; partial flows may ease acute shortages but keep volatility elevated.

events.title: OPEC+ agrees May quota increase of 206,000 bpd, largely ‘academic’ while Hormuz disruption persists
events.implication: Spare capacity cannot meaningfully offset chokepoint constraints; market remains dominated by shipping access and repair timelines.

events.title: Iraq says it could restore crude exports to ~3.4 million bpd within a week if the war ends and Hormuz reopens; says guarantees so far are verbal
events.implication: Reopening would quickly add barrels, but dependence on Iranian permissions leaves downside risk to realized exports and reinforces volatility.

events.title: Qatar declares force majeure on LNG shipments; damage reduces export capacity and shuts Shell’s Pearl GTL plant
events.implication: Sustains LNG tightness and heightens cross-commodity contagion (gas-to-oil switching), adding to global inflation and risk-off sensitivity.

links.from: Iran warns Strait of Hormuz remains closed to vessels without permits; shippers and major liners remain cautious
links.why: Permit-based transit and threats to target ships without permission keep physical flow risk and war-risk premia elevated.

links.from: U.S. strikes military targets at Kharg Island as war-driven energy disruptions mount (IEA cites 40+ damaged assets)
links.why: Strikes near export infrastructure raise probability of further outages and retaliation affecting supply.
Higher energy prices worsen import bills and inflation for net energy importers, pressuring EM FX.
Energy-driven inflation shock and geopolitical escalation typically increase demand for safe-haven assets and reduce risk appetite.
Risk-off episodes commonly widen credit spreads and raise funding costs, especially in HY/EM.

links.from: U.S., Iran and Israel announce a two-week ceasefire brokered by Pakistan; talks planned to pursue longer-term settlement
links.to: Iran warns Strait of Hormuz remains closed to vessels without permits; shippers and major liners remain cautious
links.why: Ceasefire announcement did not translate into unconditional reopening; Iran still requires permits and shippers remain cautious.

links.from: Selective ‘friendly’ transits continue: Japanese LNG carrier, French-owned CMA CGM ship, and Omani-operated tankers cross the Strait of Hormuz
links.to: Iran warns Strait of Hormuz remains closed to vessels without permits; shippers and major liners remain cautious
links.why: These crossings illustrate the same selective/permit-based passage regime described by shipowners and authorities.

links.from: Drone strike hits BP-operated North Rumaila oilfield; three workers wounded (no immediate operational impact reported)
links.to: Iraq says it could restore crude exports to ~3.4 million bpd within a week if the war ends and Hormuz reopens; says guarantees so far are verbal
links.why: Spillover attacks on Iraqi facilities and logistics can slow ramp-up even if maritime access improves.

links.from: Qatar declares force majeure on LNG shipments; damage reduces export capacity and shuts Shell’s Pearl GTL plant
links.why: LNG shortages can increase oil demand via switching and raise broader energy-complex risk premia.

links.from: OPEC+ agrees May quota increase of 206,000 bpd, largely ‘academic’ while Hormuz disruption persists
links.why: Additional quotas do not translate into deliverable barrels while chokepoint and infrastructure constraints bind.
